---
title: "Sentinel-1 (SAR)"
subtitle: "<img src='images/Sentinel-1_pillars.png' style='width:600px;'><br/>The European Radar Observatory"
author: "Jaiying LI"
institute: "CASA0023"
date: "2026/02/11"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      ratio: "16:9"
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
---

layout: true
<div class="my-header"><img src="images/Sentinel-1_pillars.png" style="width: 150px;" /></div>


---
fold Panel
上文下图

# Copernicus: Sentinel-1 — The SAR Imaging Constellation

### What is Copernicus Programme?
The Sentinel-1 mission is the European Radar Observatory for the Copernicus joint initiative of the European Commission (EC) and the European Space Agency (ESA). Copernicus is a European initiative to implement information services dealing with the environment and security based on observation data from Earth Observation satellites and ground-based information.
[https://sentiwiki.copernicus.eu/web/s1-mission#Sentinel-1-Mission]

image: "D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\week2\Copernicus Programme.png"  [[Credits: ESA]:https://sentiwiki.copernicus.eu/web/copernicus-programme ]

### What is Sentinel-1?

The Sentinels are a fleet of European Space Agency (ESA) satellites designed to acquire measurements from multiple sensor types that will provide information necessary to meet Europe's Copernicus program objectives. 

The first mission in the series, the Sentinel-1 constellation, includes twin satellites that each carry C-band synthetic aperture radar (SAR) and together provide all-weather, day-and-night imagery of Earth's surface. Sentinel-1A was launched on April 3, 2014, and Sentinel-1B on 25 April 2016. They orbit 180° apart, together imaging the Earth every six days.

[https://www.earthdata.nasa.gov/data/platforms/space-based-platforms/sentinel-1]

image: "D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\week2\Artist's view of the deployed Sentinel-1 spacecraft.jpeg" [image credit: ESA, TAS-I https://www.eoportal.org/satellite-missions/copernicus-sentinel-1#copernicus-sentinel-1--the-sar-imaging-constellation-for-land-and-ocean-services]

### What is SAR?


SAR systems are distinguished in the remote sensing world for their ability to deliver clear, high-resolution images in any environment. Unlike passive systems, SAR is an “active” sensing technology, meaning it produces its own energy and measures the reflected signals back from the Earth’s surface. This allows SAR to overcome limitations such as low visibility and adverse weather, making it a powerful tool for diverse industries, from environmental science to defense. 

[https://gno-sys.com/selecting-your-system-synthetic-aperture-radar-sar-technology/]

image: "D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\week2\SAR.png" [https://gno-sys.com/selecting-your-system-synthetic-aperture-radar-sar-technology/]


---

# Sentinel-1 Radar Mission

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FJWzLxdSMyA&t=2s]
---

# Mission Profile

上 panel + table 


## Info
Mission Name,"Copernicus Sentinel-1 (Constellation of S1A, S1B, S1C, S1D)"
Agency,ESA; COM
Mission Status,Operational (S1A: Extended; S1B: Ended; S1C: Launched/Commissioning)
Launch Dates,S1A: Apr 2014 | S1B: Apr 2016 | S1C: Dec 2024
Design Life,7.25 Years (with consumables for 12 years)

## Specifications

Resolution
25 x 40 m, 5 x 5 m, and 5 x 20 m

Spectral
1 nm-180nm

## Orbits
Orbit Type,"Sun-synchronous, Near-polar, Dawn-dusk (18:00 LTAN)"
Orbit Altitude,693 km
Inclination,98.18°
Repeat Cycle,12 days (per satellite); 6 days (combined constellation)

## Instruments
Primary Instrument,C-SAR (C-band Synthetic Aperture Radar)
Center Frequency,5.405 GHz (C-band)
Polarization,"Single (HH, VV) & Dual (HH+HV, VV+VH)"
Mass,"~2,300 kg (at launch)"
Data Policy,"Free, Full, and Open (Copernicus Data Policy)"

下图：

image 1： Schematic View of the Deployed Sentinel-1 Spacecraft. [Credits: TAS-I]  "D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\week2\schematic View of the Deployed Sentinel-1 Spacecraft.png" [https://sentiwiki.copernicus.eu/web/s1-mission#Sentinel-1-Mission]


image 2：Sentinel-1 Constellation [Credits: ESA/ATG medialab] "D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\week2\orbit.png" [https://sentiwiki.copernicus.eu/web/s1-mission]




---

# Technical Specifications - Sensor Modes

Copernicus Sentinel-1 operates in four exclusive acquisition modes:


table:
Mode,Swath Width,Resolution (Rg x Az),Primary Use Case
IW (Interferometric Wide Swath),250 km,5 x 20 m,Main operational mode over land; InSAR.
EW (Extra Wide Swath),400 km,20 x 40 m,"Maritime surveillance, sea-ice, and polar zones."
SM (Stripmap),80 km,5 x 5 m,High-resolution for emergency/special cases.
WV (Wave),20 x 20 km,5 x 5 m,Sampled every 100km for ocean wind/waves.

Stripmap (SM)
Interferometric Wide swath (IW) - this is the main data acquisition mode of Sentinel-1, TOPSAR (Terrain Observation with Progressive Scans SAR)
Extra-Wide swath (EW)

image: Overview of the Sentinel-1 C-SAR instrument observation scheme and operational support modes (image credit: ESA) "D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\week2\modes.jpeg" [https://www.eoportal.org/satellite-missions/copernicus-sentinel-1#c-sar-c-band-sar-instrument]


---
# Mechanisms - SAR

panel， image相应变换

## Active sensing

A sensor can be classified as either passive or active. A passive sensor, using optical systems, records electromagnetic waves emitted by the sun and reflected from the ground surface. An active sensor, used by SAR systems, functions as both the source and the receiver. This means the sensor transmits the electromagnetic waves and also records the reflected waves. Unlike an optical sensor, a SAR sensor can operate during the day or night, independent of the sun, since it transmits its own signal.

image: An optical sensor (left) only works with clear, sunny skies. A SAR sensor (right) can work day and night, and even when clouds are present. "D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\week2\active.png" [https://pro.arcgis.com/en/pro-app/latest/help/analysis/image-analyst/introduction-to-synthetic-aperture-radar.htm]

## Polarization

SAR transmits electromagnetic waves; the orientation of an electromagnetic wave is called polarisation. Every electromagnetic wave consists of electric and magnetic fields. Horizontal polarisation is denoted as H and vertical polarisation is denoted as V. There are four common polarisation combinations used in SAR sensors: 

HH: Sensor transmits a horizontal signal and receives a horizontal signal. 
VV: Sensor transmits a vertical signal and receives a vertical signal. 
HV: Sensor transmits a horizontal signal and receives a vertical signal. 
VH: Sensor transmits a vertical signal and receives a horizontal signal. 

Vertical polarisation is used to detect elevated objects, such as buildings, rough sea or transmission towers. Horizontal polarisation is more suitable for flat and low surfaces – rivers, bridges, or for example power lines. 


image："D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\week2\Polarization.png"[https://pro.arcgis.com/en/pro-app/latest/help/analysis/image-analyst/introduction-to-synthetic-aperture-radar.htm]

## Spatial resolution

As the satellite orbits the earth, the sensor images the ground multiple times. Combining this with signal processing techniques allows a SAR sensor (with a short antenna) to synthetically elongate its antenna to get a larger aperture.

image："D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\week2\GUID-9D716342-931F-4B75-B896-9ECE1FF68719-web.gif" [https://pro.arcgis.com/en/pro-app/latest/help/analysis/image-analyst/introduction-to-synthetic-aperture-radar.htm]

---
# Comparison - Optical and SAR
Feature,Optical Imagery,SAR Imagery
Energy Source,Passive (Relies on Sunlight),Active (Emits its own Radar pulses)
Illumination,Requires daylight,Works 24/7 (Day and Night)
Atmospheric Effect,"Blocked by clouds, smoke, and haze","Penetrates clouds, smoke, and light rain"
Image Basis,Reflectance of visible/infrared light,Backscatter of microwave energy
Image： Color, size, number, form, etc., of the object ；Structure and material of target objects
    (artifacts/natural objects)

image: SAR vs Optical satellites. Source: [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ecfkPuSe45Y] "D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\week2\sar_optical.png"


---
# Sentinel-1 C-SAR Application Portfolio

.scroll-box[

| Category | Application | Key Features & Technical Utility | Source Reference / Article | URL |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Land Monitoring** | **Forestry** | Mapping forest extent and monitoring deforestation/degradation using C-band backscatter. | SOFT: Synergetic Operational Forest Monitoring | [Link](https://eo4society.esa.int/projects/soft/) |
| **Land Monitoring** | **Agriculture** | Monitoring phenological cycles and crop classification through multi-temporal VV/VH ratios. | Crop changes over one year | [Link](https://www.esa.int/ESA_Multimedia/Images/2021/02/Crop_changes_over_one_year) |
| **Land Monitoring** | **Urban Deformation** | Utilizing InSAR to detect structural integrity and ground stability following major incidents. | Beirut explosion: radar analysis | [Link](https://www.earthstartsbeating.com/2020/08/11/beirutexplosion/) |
| **Land Monitoring** | **Landscape Topography** | Generating high-resolution digital elevation models and global backscatter mosaics (S1GBM). | Sentinel-1 Global Backscatter Model | [Link](https://sentiwiki.copernicus.eu/web/s1-products#Sentinel-1-Global-Backscatter-Model-(S1GBM)) |
| **Land Monitoring** | **Soil Moisture** | Retrieval of Surface Soil Moisture (SSM) by analyzing sensitivity of backscatter to water content. | Copernicus Global Land: Soil Moisture | [Link](https://land.copernicus.eu/global/products/ssm) |
| **Land Monitoring** | **Vegetation** | Tracking vegetation optical depth and biomass changes via time-series SAR indices. | SentiWiki: Vegetation Monitoring | [Link](https://sentiwiki.copernicus.eu/web/s1-applications#Vegetation) |
| **Maritime Monitoring** | **Ice Monitoring** | Tracking sea-ice concentration, type, and iceberg drift to ensure safe polar navigation. | Sentinel-1: Antarctic sea ice and sheets | [Link](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cpYrnP_B3qc) |
| **Maritime Monitoring** | **Ship Monitoring** | Near real-time detection of vessels and shipping lanes, regardless of AIS transponder status. | Bay of Naples: Shipping from Space | [Link](https://www.esa.int/ESA_Multimedia/Images/2020/07/Bay_of_Naples_Italy) |
| **Maritime Monitoring** | **Oil Pollution** | Detection of oil slicks (dampening of capillary waves) for environmental enforcement. | Oil spill spread detection | [Link](https://www.esa.int/ESA_Multimedia/Images/2018/10/Oil_spill_spread) |
| **Maritime Monitoring** | **Waves and Winds** | Extracting ocean swell spectra and wind speed vectors using SAR Wave (WV) mode. | Medicanes: Monitoring Numa | [Link](https://www.earthstartsbeating.com/2017/11/24/medicanes-numa/) |
| **Emergency Management** | **Flood Monitoring** | Rapid mapping of water-covered areas during extreme weather events and cloud cover. | Copernicus Emergency Management Service | [Link](https://emergency.copernicus.eu/) |
| **Emergency Management** | **Earthquake Analysis** | Mapping co-seismic surface displacement and fault lines using D-InSAR techniques. | Türkiye/Syria Earthquakes impact | [Link](https://www.esa.int/Applications/Observing_the_Earth/Satellites_support_impact_assessment_after_Tuerkiye_Syria_earthquakes) |
| **Emergency Management** | **Landslide / Volcano** | Monitoring pre-eruptive ground inflation and slope stability to mitigate geological risks. | Evaluating ground deformation via S-1 | [Link](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/using-sentinel-1-satellite-evaluate-ground-deformation-caused-/) |
]


* **Copernicus SentiWiki**: [Sentinel-1 Applications Guide](https://sentiwiki.copernicus.eu/web/s1-applications)
* **Scientific Data**: Bauer-Marschallinger, B., Cao, S., Navacchi, C. et al. *The normalised Sentinel-1 Global Backscatter Model, mapping Earth’s land surface with C-band microwaves*. Sci Data 8, 277 (2021). [https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-021-01059-7](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-021-01059-7)


<style>
.scroll-box {
height: 450px;
overflow-y: scroll;
display: block;
}
/* Optional: ensures the table takes full width inside the scroll box */
.scroll-box table {
width: 100%;
}
</style>

---
# Reflection - Advantages and limitations of SAR imagery


Advantages
All-weather capability: As an active sensor operating in the microwave spectrum, SAR can penetrate clouds, smoke, rain, and fog to provide reliable data
.
Day-and-night operation: It provides its own energy source, enabling consistent Earth observation regardless of solar illumination
.
Material penetration: Microwaves can penetrate through foliage and dry soil to reveal underlying structures
.
High spatial resolution and revisit rates: Modern SAR can achieve resolutions down to 25cm and frequent revisits, which is critical for monitoring rapid changes like urban expansion
.


Limitations
High technical complexity: Processing SAR data requires specialized software and expertise due to its unique geometric and physical properties
.
Speckle noise: SAR imagery is inherently affected by grainy "salt and pepper" noise, which can obscure fine details and complicate classification
.
Physical sensitivity: Backscatter intensity is highly sensitive to surface roughness and moisture content (dielectric constant) rather than traditional color
.
Lack of intuitive visualization: The resulting grayscale imagery is not as visually interpretable as optical data and often requires background context to identify features
.


Reflection
Combining SAR and optical imagery
While SAR images can see through clouds and provide valuable information about the surface characteristics, optical imagery brings clearer details and colours. However, this doesn’t mean you have to choose between SAR and optical imagery. It’s not one or the other – they are perfectly complementary and the combination of both will provide you with the unique possibility to monitor areas of your interested under all conditions and with the highest amount of detail available. 

GIS layers
GIS layers are digital representations of geographic features used in Geographic Information Systems (GIS) for spatial analysis. When integrated with SAR, GIS layers provide contextual information such as land cover types, infrastructure, and boundaries, making the interpretation and analysis of radar data easier and more accurate.  

image：Same spatial resolution….for different pixel sizes "D:\0.CASA_USS\Jiaying-2026 portfolio-book\images\week2\imagesar.png" [https://elisecolin.medium.com/why-a-sar-image-is-not-your-usual-optical-one-2ee3f199c5ef]
